with your audience. a well-developed speaking style helps you connect with your audience on an emotional level and captures their attention. there is an unlimited speaking style for every occasion. for example, the way you speak at a wedding is different from the way you will speak in the church. a motivational speaker for example cannot deliver speech in a flat tone .he should use a sense of mystery and drama in his speech, add energy and speaking quickly styles work for him. but for a command style speaker, seriousness,speaking slowly, making deliberate and smooth movements work. 3. body language:nonverbal communication, including body language, plays a significant role in public speaking. your posture, gestures, and movements can convey confidence, passion, and engagement. effective body language helps to reinforce your message, create rapport, and establish a connection with the audience. posture: a good posture is essential to appear confident and authoritative. stand tall with your shoulders relaxed, back straight, and feet planted firmly on the ground. eye contact: maintaining eye contact with the audience helps establish a connection and convey confidence. make sure to scan the entire audience and hold eye contact with different individuals. facial expressions: your facial expressions should reflect the emotions and messages you're conveying. smile genuinely, use appropriate expressions to convey enthusiasm, and exhibit a range of emotions depending on the content of your speech. gestures: purposeful and natural gestures can enhance your speech and make it more engaging. use your hands and arms to illustrate key points, emphasize important words, and create visual impact. avoid fidgeting or excessive gestures that may distract the audience.

movement: controlled movement across the stage helps maintain the audience's attention and ensures they stay engaged. walk confidently, and use the stage to your advantage, but avoid pacing back and forth or excessive movement that can be distracting. voice and tone: your body language also includes how you use your voice and tone. speak clearly and confidently, vary your pitch and tone to add emphasis, and use pauses effectively to create impact. nervous habits: be mindful of any nervous habits or involuntary movements that may detract from your presentation. avoid fidgeting, excessive hand movements, playing with your hair, or any other distracting behaviors. remember to practice and rehearse your speech, paying attention to your body language. while it's important to be aware of your body language, it's equally essential to be authentic and let your personality shine through. 4. facial expressions:facial expressions are a powerful form of nonverbal communication in public speaking. they can help convey emotions, engage the audience, and add depth to your message. here are some tips for effectively using facial expressions during a speech: smile: a warm and genuine smile can instantly create a connection with your audience. it conveys friendliness, approachability, and positivity. use a smile to help set a positive and welcoming tone for your speech. eye contact: make sure to maintain eye contact with your audience and vary your gaze. look directly into the eyes of individuals in the audience to create a sense of connection and engagement. use your eyes to express emotions such as enthusiasm, empathy, or concern. brow movement: your eyebrows can communicate different emotions and add emphasis to your speech. raising your eyebrows can show surprise or excitement, while furrowing them can convey seriousness or concern. use these movements intentionally to emphasize important points or add emotional impact to your speech. facial variations: allow your face to naturally respond to the content of your speech. for example, if you are sharing a funny anecdote, let your face light up with a smile or chuckle. if discussing something serious or sad, maintain a more sombre expression. by authentically reflecting the emotions of your message, you can engage and connect with your audience on a deeper level. avoid monotony: just as with your voice, you want to avoid a monotone facial expression. vary your facial expressions to reflect the different emotions, moods, and messages you are conveying throughout your speech. this will help keep the audience interested and engaged. practice in front of a mirror or camera: to improve your facial expressions, practice in front of a mirror or record yourself speaking. pay attention to how your facial expressions align with your message and adjust them as needed. this can help you become more aware of any habits or expressions that might distract from your speech. remember, the goal is to be authentic and genuine in your facial expressions. be mindful of your facial expressions to effectively convey your message and connect with your audience. * * * * * *

conflict within a police department can arise from various factors. here are some common reasons for conflict in police departments: 1. personality clashes: personalities within a police department can differ greatly, and conflicting personalities can cause friction and interpersonal conflict. 2. power struggles: conflict can arise when there is a perceived or real power struggle within the department. competition for promotions, authority, or control can lead to conflicts between officers or between different ranks within the department. 3. lack of communication: poor communication or miscommunication can be a significant source of conflict. when information is not effectively shared, misunderstandings can occur, leading to tension and disagreements. 4. differences in values and work styles: police officers come from diverse backgrounds and may have different values, work styles, or approaches to policing. these differences can lead to conflicts as officers may struggle to find common ground or clash over differing perspectives. 5. resource allocation: conflicts may arise when there are limited resources or competing demands for resources within the department. officers may have different opinions on how resources should be distributed, leading to tension and disputes. 6. organizational culture and policies: differences in the organizational culture or policies of a police department can contribute to conflict. if officers perceive policies as unfair or ineffective, conflicts may arise between officers and department leadership. 7. personal issues outside of work: officers, like anyone else, can experience personal issues or challenges outside of work. these personal issues can sometimes spill over into the workplace and contribute to conflicts. 8. ethical dilemmas: police work often presents officers with ethical dilemmas, and differing opinions on what is the right course of action can lead to conflicts within the department. it is important to address and manage conflicts within a police department to maintain a healthy work environment and ensure effective law enforcement. this can involve open communication, conflict resolution strategies, training on conflict management, and a commitment to fostering a positive organizational culture. interpersonal conflict refers to any type of conflict involving two or more people. mild or severe, interpersonal conflict is a natural outcome of human interaction. people have very different personalities, values, expectations, and attitudes toward problem-solving. when you work or interact with someone who doesn't share your opinions or goals, conflict can result. - first identify the conflict. - pseudo conflict -a pseudo conflict typically happens in one of the following situations: - a misunderstanding leads to a difference of opinion. - the people involved in the conflict believe they have different goals when, in reality, they have similar goals. - when one person involved in the conflict mocks or taunts the other (sometimes called badgering). - in most cases, you can resolve pseudo conflict without too much trouble. it generally just takes a bit of clarification about what you actually meant or some further exploration of how your goals actually do align. - - fact conflict -you're utterly convinced snakes can hear, but your friend insists they can't because they don't have ears. this illustrates a fact conflict, also called simple conflict. fact conflict happens when two or more people disagree over information or the

- truth of something. because this kind of conflict involves facts, you can often resolve it pretty easily. all you have to do is check a credible source for the truth. - - value conflict this kind of conflict comes up when different personal values lead to disagreement. if you and a co-worker have different views on abortion rights, for example, or you and your brother have different religious beliefs, you might find yourself in a value conflict. this type of conflict doesn't always have a clear path to resolution. people can have such widely varying personal values and beliefs, so you may find it most helpful to just acknowledge your opposing viewpoints (respectfully) and accept that you likely won't change each other's minds. - - policy conflict this conflict happens when people can't agree on a problem-solving strategy or action plan in a given situation. personality, upbringing, education, and any number of other factors might have an impact on someone's approach to policy, or problemsolving, so this kind of conflict isn't unusual. it might happen when parents disagree on the most effective way to discipline a child, for example, or when co-workers have different ideas about the best way to tackle a big project. - - ego conflict ever had an argument where neither you nor the other person involved could back down or accept a loss? ego conflict often develops alongside other types of conflict, and it can make any disagreement trickier to navigate. it commonly happens when conflict gets personal. may be you, or others involved, link the outcome of conflict to your intelligence. or perhaps someone uses the disagreement as a platform to make judgmental or derogatory remarks. in either scenario, attempts to resolve the actual conflict might derail as you concentrate on the ego conflict instead. - - meta conflict meta conflict happens when you have conflict about your conflicts. some examples:- - 'you always nod along, but you never actually hear what i'm saying!' - 'that's so unfair. that's not what we're talking about at all.' - 'you're too worked up. i can't deal with you when you're like this.' to resolve conflict effectively, you need to communicate clearly. while meta conflict might bring up issues with communication, it often does so in unhelpful ways. 1. collaborating style: this style involves actively seeking a win-win solution by involving all parties in the conflict. it focuses on open communication, listening to each other's perspectives, and finding mutually beneficial solutions. 2. accommodating style: this style involves prioritizing the needs and concerns of the other party over one's own. this approach is often used when preserving the relationship is more important than achieving personal goals or when one party has more power or authority. 3. compromising style: this style involves finding middle ground by making concessions and giving up some of one's own interests. it aims to find a fair and reasonable solution that partially satisfies both parties. 4. avoiding style: this style involves minimizing or ignoring the conflict altogether. it may be used when the issue at hand is relatively unimportant or when a more appropriate time and place to address the conflict can be found. 5. competing style: this style involves asserting one's own interests and priorities at the expense of the other party. it entails a winlose approach and may be used when quick decisions are necessary or when one party has a stronger position of power. these styles can be effective in different situations, depending on the nature of the conflict and the individuals involved. the key is to assess the situation and choose the appropriate style that will best resolve the conflict and maintain positive relationships. when addressing conflicts involving a senior or boss, it is crucial to approach the situation with professionalism and respect. here are some strategies for managing conflict with a senior or boss: 1. choose the right time and place: find an appropriate time and private setting to discuss the conflict. avoid addressing the issue in front of others or during moments of high stress or busyness. 2. prepare and clarify your concerns: think through your concerns or grievances before the discussion. clearly identify the specific issues you want to address and gather supporting evidence or examples, if applicable. this will help ensure clarity and focus during the conversation.

3. be respectful and maintain professionalism: approach the conversation with a respectful and professional demeanor. be mindful of your tone and language, avoiding aggressive or accusatory behavior. focus on the problem at hand rather than attacking the person. 4. active listening and perspective-taking: allow your boss or senior to express their perspective and actively listen to their point of view. demonstrate empathy and try to understand their reasoning and concerns. this can help create a more constructive and open dialogue. 5. offer potential solutions: instead of simply highlighting the problem, suggest potential solutions or alternatives. this demonstrates your commitment to finding a resolution and can facilitate a more collaborative approach to problem-solving. 6. seek advice or mediation if needed: if the conflict persists or proves challenging to resolve, you may consider seeking advice from a trusted third party, such as a mentor or hr representative. they may offer guidance or mediate the discussion if necessary. 7. focus on the bigger picture: remember the overall goals and objectives of your organization or team. keep in mind that conflict resolution is about finding common ground and maintaining a positive working relationship. it is important to note that each conflict situation will be unique, and the strategies employed may vary based on individual dynamics and organizational culture. adapting these strategies to the specific circumstances is advisable. a subordinate is someone who is in a secondary rank. as a manager, leader, or supervisor, your ability to lead, manage, supervise and motivate your subordinates/staff -the people under your purview is of great importance. the following tips will help you to improve your leadership skills and relationships with your subordinates and get support from them. 1. be fair to everyone: treat each of your subordinates/staff fairly and respectfully. some people are by nature more likable than others, but as a supervisor, you have to avoid even the slightest hint of favouritism; be fair to everyone. you may have to be flexible in your leadership approach to each of your subordinates. 2. develop everyone: everyone is unique. each subordinate has unique strengths and weaknesses. so, work on developing the abilities or the potential of every employee. never overlook the need to provide the necessary resources or training for the employees to do their jobs. by doing so, they will perceive that you care for their well-being. in return, they will be more respectful to you and work better. 3. know and like your subordinates: -learn the individual strengths and weaknesses of the people you supervise. it will aid you in assigning tasks based on skills rather than randomly. it will make them feel good working with you. 4. show continuing interest in your subordinates: -show continuing concern for your subordinates by providing feedback on their performance - not just at performance evaluation time. be honest whether they are doing well or bad. it means praising when it is right - not when it isn't. 5. criticize with care: be diplomatic whenever you criticize any aspect of an employee's performance. never jump to any judgment or conclusion without thinking first about the consequences of your critics. so, be tactful to your subordinates at all times. they will surely appreciate you for being a considerate leader/manager. 6. be flexible: -be flexible in dealing with employees' concerns. going strictly by set procedures won't always give you the flexibility you need to resolve individual performance. so, always use your 6. good common sense. 7. give simple directions: give simple but specific job directions anytime you assign a new task to a worker. make things not so difficult in the eyes of your subordinates if it is a challenging task. it will make them feel good and confident doing the job. 8. defend your subordinates: defend your subordinates against unfair criticisms; be with them. make them think and feel that you are a reasonable or supportive leader/manager/supervisor. 9. be a coach - not a general: remember! you are at work, not at war. so, be loyal to your subordinates and the company/ organization/employer. invite them to the decision-making process whenever feasible. you can also show your leadership qualities by working to obtain promotions, pay raises, and awards for deserving employees.

peer conflict management involves effectively addressing and resolving conflicts between colleagues at the same level within an organization. the ways to manage peers are different from the ones used for your supervisees and supervisors. these include setting mutually beneficial goals; establishing ones credibility, persuasion skills, and the ability to tap into ones network. the skill needed to strengthen managing across is emotional intelligence, self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, and social skills. this will help better understand one's own and others priorities, pressures, and work styles. the following steps help in improving effectiveness of managing across in the organization:- - encourage open communication: create a culture of open communication where colleagues feel comfortable expressing their concerns and viewpoints. encourage active listening and respectful dialogue among peers, promoting an environment of trust and understanding. - clarify expectations and roles: ensure that everyone understands their roles, responsibilities, and expectations within the team. clearly define individual roles and tasks to minimize ambiguity and potential conflicts arising from misunderstandings or overlapping duties. - facilitate collaboration: encourage collaboration and teamwork among peers to foster a positive and cooperative work environment. emphasize the importance of shared goals and the benefits of working together to achieve them. - address conflicts early: intervene as soon as conflicts arise, rather than allowing them to escalate. addressing conflicts early can prevent them from worsening and affecting team dynamics. act as a mediator if necessary to help facilitate a constructive dialogue between the involved parties. - promote empathy and understanding: encourage peers to see situations from each other's perspectives. encourage empathy and understanding, helping colleagues recognize that conflicts often arise from different viewpoints, experiences, or priorities. foster an environment where individuals actively listen and seek to understand one another. - foster a problem-solving mindset: encourage a problem-solving approach to conflict resolution among peers. encourage them to focus on finding mutually beneficial solutions rather than focusing solely on their own interests. help them develop skills in negotiation, compromise, and collaborative problem-solving. - seek third-party assistance if needed: in some cases, conflicts may be particularly challenging to resolve among peers. in such situations, consider involving a neutral third party, such as a mediator or hr representative, to help facilitate the resolution process. their objective perspective can provide valuable guidance and support. - encourage ongoing professional development: promote continuous learning and development among peers. provide opportunities for skill-building and training in areas such as conflict resolution, communication, and emotional intelligence. strengthening these skills can enhance conflict management abilities and contribute to a positive work environment. interdepartmental conflict is a type of conflict that occurs between different departments or units within an organization. it can arise from various sources, such as competing goals, scarce resources, unclear roles, communication breakdowns, or personality clashes. interdepartmental conflict can have negative impacts on the organization, such as reduced productivity, lower morale, increased turnover, or damaged reputation. to effectively manage interdepartmental conflict, a combination of proactive and reactive strategies should be employed. proactive strategies, such as establishing clear goals, roles, and expectations, fostering collaboration and trust, providing adequate resources and support, and creating opportunities for interdepartmental interaction, are designed to prevent or minimize the occurrence of conflict. reactive strategies, on the other hand, involve identifying the sources and nature of the conflict, using a constructive and respectful approach to communicate and negotiate, seeking a win-win solution, implementing and monitoring the agreed solution, and learning from the conflict.

by utilizing both proactive and reactive strategies, organizations can ensure that interdepartmental conflict is managed in a way that is beneficial to all parties involved. organizational culture is the set of shared beliefs, values, norms, and practices that shape the behaviour and interactions of the members of an organization. it influences how the organization defines and handles conflict, as well as how the members perceive and respond to conflict. for example, an organizational culture that values innovation, diversity, and learning may encourage constructive conflict as a source of creativity and improvement, while an organizational culture that values stability, conformity, and harmony may discourage or suppress conflict as a source of disruption and threat. benefits include increased productivity and efficiency, as it reduces the waste of time, energy, and resources caused by conflict, and increases coordination and cooperation. quality and innovation can also be improved, as it stimulates the generation and exchange of diverse and creative ideas and solutions, and encourages the adoption and implementation of best practices and standards. morale and satisfaction are also heightened, as it creates a positive and supportive work environment, and increases the engagement and motivation of the members. additionally, it can reduce turnover and absenteeism, as it retains and attracts talented and committed members, and reduces the stress and burnout caused by conflict. finally, it can enhance the reputation and image of the organization, by demonstrating its professionalism and competence, and by building and maintaining positive relationships with external stakeholders and customers. interdepartmental conflict resolution can be a powerful tool to improve organizational culture, as it can foster respect, trust, collaboration, communication, and learning. by acknowledging and appreciating the differences and similarities among departments and individuals, and treating each other with dignity and courtesy, it can build and maintain credibility, reliability, and openness. furthermore, it encourages working together toward common goals, sharing information and resources, and supporting and complementing each other's strengths and weaknesses. additionally, it promotes expressing and listening to each other's ideas, opinions, and feelings, and providing and receiving constructive feedback and suggestions. finally, it seeks and offers opportunities for interdepartmental learning and development, and applies the lessons learned from conflict to improve future performance and outcomes. controlling anger and aggression at work is essential for maintaining a positive and productive work environment. here are some strategies to help manage and control anger and aggression in the workplace: 1. self-awareness: recognize and acknowledge your emotions, including feelings of anger or aggression. pay attention to physical and emotional cues that indicate rising anger, such as increased heart rate or tension in your body. being aware of your emotions enables you to take proactive steps to manage them. 2. practice deep breathing and relaxation techniques: when you feel anger or aggression building up, take a moment to step away from the situation and engage in deep breathing exercises or relaxation techniques. deep breathing helps activate the body's relaxation response, reducing the intensity of anger or aggression. 3. count to ten and pause: before responding to a provoking situation or engaging in a conflict, take a pause and count to ten. this brief moment gives you time to calm down and think before reacting impulsively. 4. identify triggers and manage stress: reflect on situations or people that tend to trigger your anger or aggression at work. once identified, explore strategies to manage stress and minimize triggers. this may involve practicing stress management techniques, setting boundaries, or seeking support from colleagues or supervisors. 5. communicate assertively: instead of resorting to aggressive or confrontational behaviour, use assertive communication techniques

to express your needs, concerns, and boundaries clearly. express yourself calmly, using 'i' statements to convey how you feel or how a specific situation is affecting you. 6. seek support from colleagues or supervisors: if you find it challenging to control anger or aggression at work, consider reaching out for support. talk to trusted colleagues or supervisors who can provide guidance, advice, or a listening ear. they may be able to offer perspective or solutions to manage difficult situations. 7. take regular breaks and practice self-care: take breaks throughout the workday to recharge and practice self-care. engage in activities that help relax and rejuvenate you, such as going for a walk, listening to calming music, or practicing mindfulness exercises. prioritize self-care as a means to reduce stress and maintain emotional well-being. 8. seek professional help if needed: if anger and aggression persist and significantly impact your work or relationships, consider seeking professional help. a therapist or counsellor can provide tools and strategies to effectively manage anger and aggression in a professional setting. remember, controlling anger and aggression at work requires ongoing effort and self-reflection. by implementing these strategies and seeking support when needed, you can create a more positive and harmonious work environment for yourself and those around you. * * * * * * dharna, a form of protest in south asia, has been a powerful tool for social and political change. with the advent of social media, the dissemination of dharna has become more widespread and accessible, leading to a greater impact on society. in this article, we will explore the impact of social media on dharna and its dissemination in contemporary times. social media has revolutionized the way people communicate and share information. it has become a powerful tool for organizing and mobilizing people for social and political causes, including dharna. social media platforms like twitter and facebook allow protesters to share information about their cause, organize events, and reach a wider audience. the use of social media has also made it easier for people to participate in dharna, regardless of their physical location. this has led to a greater diversity of voices and perspectives being heard in the protest movement. while social media has provided new opportunities for dharna, it has also presented new challenges. the ease with which information can be shared on social media has led to the spread of misinformation and fake news, which can undermine the credibility of the protest movement. additionally, the use of social media has made it easier for authorities to monitor and track protesters, which can lead to arrests and other forms of repression. however, social media can also be used to raise awareness about these issues and to mobilize support for the protest movement.

police negotiations engaged with the leaders of the protest and listened to their grievances. through a series of negotiations, a compromise was reached, and the protest was peacefully ended. case study 2: jnu student protest in 2019, student at jawaharlal nehru university (jnu) in delhi protested against a proposed fee hike. the police were called in to control the protest, but instead of using force, they engaged in negotiations with the student leaders. through a series of discussions, a compromise was reached, and the protest was peacefully ended. successful police negotiations during dharnas can have a significant impact on reducing violence and promoting peaceful resolutions. by listening to the grievances of the protesters and engaging in discussions, the police can build trust and find a compromise that satisfies both parties. this approach can also help to maintain law and order while respecting the right to protest. 2. when a dharna or protest involves a dead body being placed on the road, the situation becomes highly sensitive and requires careful handling. here are some considerations for police negotiation in such circumstances: 1. prioritize safety and public order: the primary responsibility of the police is to ensure the safety and security of the public. assess the situation to determine if the protest poses any immediate threats or disruptions to public order. if there are public safety concerns, address them promptly. 2. engage in dialogue : establish communication channels with the protest organizers or designated representatives to understand their demands and concerns. attempt to engage in a constructive dialogue to find a peaceful resolution. 3. show empathy and sensitivity: recognize the emotional nature of the situation, as the presence of a dead body can intensify emotions and grievances. display empathy and sensitivity during the negotiation process, acknowledging the distress of the protestors while maintaining professionalism. 4. respect cultural and religious considerations: if the presence of the dead body on the road is due to cultural or religious beliefs, consider the sentiments associated with it. sensitivity towards these cultural and religious practices can aid in facilitating a peaceful resolution.

5. mediation and conflict resolution : in complex situations involving a dead body, mediation may be beneficial. trained mediators or community leaders can help facilitate dialogue between the protestors, police, and relevant authorities, aiming to find a mutually agreeable solution. 6. legal considerations: be aware of the legal implications surrounding the handling of deceased bodies, public order, and protests in your jurisdiction. adhere to applicable laws and regulations while engaging in negotiation and decision-making processes. 7. document the negotiations: maintain clear records and documentation of the negotiation process, including discussions, agreements, and any promises made. this can provide transparency, prevent misunderstandings, and ensure accountability on both sides. 8. seek assistance from relevant authorities : if required, seek guidance from legal advisors, local government officials, or higher-ranking police officers to navigate the complexities of the situation and determine the appropriate course of action. the negotiation process in this scenario should aim to strike a balance between respecting the sentiments of the protestors, ensuring public safety, and upholding legal obligations. collaboratively seeking a peaceful resolution can significantly contribute to resolving the situation effectively. kidnapping for ransom is a serious criminal offense where a person is unlawfully taken against their will, with the intention of demanding a ransom in exchange for their release. when such incidents occur, it is crucial for law enforcement agencies to promptly intervene and initiate negotiations with the kidnappers to secure the safe release of the victim. here's an outline of the typical process and strategies employed during negotiations: 1. reporting the kidnapping : the first step is for the victim's family, friends, or colleagues to report the kidnapping to the local authorities. the police will initiate an investigation and assemble a specialized team to handle the case. 2. gathering information: the police will gather as much information as possible about the victim, including their background, relationships, employment, and any other relevant details. this information helps in understanding the motives of the kidnappers and developing negotiation strategies. 3. establishing contact : the police will work to establish contact with the kidnappers using various communication channels. this could involve using the victim's phone, a negotiator's phone, or even an intermediary. 4. building trust: negotiators aim to establish trust with the kidnappers by displaying empathy, patience, and understanding. they assure the kidnappers of their sincerity in trying to resolve the situation peacefully while focusing on the safe release of the victim. 5. negotiating ransom amount and terms: the kidnappers often demand a specific ransom amount or other conditions for the victim's release. negotiators cautiously work to lower the ransom amount or negotiate alternative terms, such as staged payments or the provision of specific items instead of money. case study:the rachakonda police rescued a 4 year old girl who was kidnapped in ghatkesar, a satellite city of hyderabad on july 7th 2023. the child krishnaveni was safely handed over to her parents by the police within hours of her kidnapping. the police said that the accused, identified as suresh was a neighbour and abducted the child at 8:00 pm on july 6 th , when she was out to buy a chocolate. the police responded immediately upon receiving the complaint and started search operations through special teams. all the cctv cameras in the vicinity were checked and the moments of the accused were noticed. the accused was taken into custody at the secunderabad railway station. the man told the police that he had kidnapped the girl to sell her to some childless couple. 6. verification of life and proof of identity: to ensure that the victim is still alive, kidnappers may provide either photographic evidence, voice recordings, or allow brief direct communication.

negotiators use this opportunity to verify the victim's condition, seeks proof of identity, and maintain contact. 7. locating the kidnappers: throughout the negotiations, the police will employ various techniques, including tracing phone calls, determining the location via triangulation, or seeking assistance from telecommunication providers, to pinpoint the kidnappers' whereabouts. 8. coordinating a safe handover: once the negotiation reaches a resolution, the police will work on setting up a location and time for the victim's release. this process requires careful planning, coordination with relevant personnel, and ensuring the safety of those involved. 9. arrest and prosecution: once the victim is safely released, the police will aim to apprehend the kidnappers and bring them to justice. this could involve surveillance, coordinated raids, or other strategic operations to prevent their escape or subsequent criminal activities. it is important to note that each kidnapping case is unique, and the specifics of negotiation and police operations may vary depending on the circumstances, jurisdiction, and resources available. additionally, involvement from experts such as hostage negotiators, intelligence analysts, and forensic specialists often aids in successful negotiations and resolving such crimes swiftly and safely. when it comes to police negotiations with the media , it is essential for both parties to understand and respect each other's roles and responsibilities. here are some considerations for a productive and professional negotiation: 1. establish open lines of communication : foster a positive and ongoing relationship with local law enforcement and their designated press officers. maintain regular contact to establish trust, clarify expectations, and address any concerns. 2. understand each other's objectives: the police aim to serve the public interest and maintain public safety, while the media's role is to inform and provide accurate news. recognize that these objectives can align or sometimes conflict, but both should strive for transparency and accountability. 3. early information sharing: police can provide media with timely and accurate information to keep the public well-informed, as long as it doesn't jeopardize ongoing investigations or compromise public safety. establish protocols for sharing initial details while respecting the need for confidentiality when required. 4. respect operational boundaries: police departments may have policies or legal restrictions on what information they can disclose during an active investigation. understand that limitations may exist to protect the integrity of the case or the privacy of individuals involved. 5. ensure fairness and balance: the media should present balanced and unbiased reporting, avoiding sensationalism or speculative information that could harm an ongoing investigation or the reputation of individuals involved. likewise, the police should provide accurate and reliable information without withholding crucial details that the public ought to know. 6. consider press conferences and statements : the police may conduct press conferences or issue statements to address the media and public inquiries related to significant incidents. collaborate on organizing these events to ensure meaningful access and an opportunity for journalists to ask relevant questions. 7. adhere to legal and ethical guidelines: both police and media personnel should be familiar with laws, regulations, and ethical standards regarding reporting and police communications. respect privacy, avoid undue harm, and ensure responsible coverage. by maintaining open lines of communication, fostering mutual understanding, and respecting each other's professional responsibilities, negotiation between police and the media can lead to productive collaboration that benefits the public. custodial deaths, especially ones receiving significant media attention, present a unique challenge for police-media negotiation. striking a balance between transparency, the public's right to know, and the investigative process is crucial. open and ongoing communication between the police and media can facilitate a more effective negotiation process while upholding the principles of accountability and justice. case study 1 :-on june 16, father balashouri, from govindapur village, lodged a complaint with the addagudur police station alleging that rs 2 lakh was stolen from his church-cum-residence. mariyamma was accused of stealing the money and was arrested on the night of june 17.

police also recovered rs 90,000 cash from the suspects. while the trio was being interrogated at the police station, mariamma suddenly complained of uneasiness and she was shifted to bhongir area hospital, where she was declared brought dead. after mariamma's death on june 18 th , at the bhongir area hospital, when she was in police custody, as per procedure, a postmortem was conducted and a custodial death case was registered under section 176 of crpc against the si and 2 constables. on june 22 nd , rachakonda commissioner suspended the addagudur si and the 2 constables for dereliction of duties in relation to the custodial death of mariamma. later , they were dismissed from service under article 311 (2)(b) of constitution and 25 (2) of conduct rules on july 20 th by the cp,rachakonda. case study 2:'custodial death' of a father and son in sathankulam town in tamil nadu's thoothukudi district has led to protests. - the deceased have been identified as p. jayaraj (58), a timber trader, and his son, j. benicks, 31. - they ran a mobile phone service and sales centre in sattankulam town in thoothukudi district. - on june 19, 2020, jayaraj was in the mobile phone showroom of his son benicks. - personnel from the sathankulam police station were on patrol duty in the evening. - the police picked him up for allegedly keeping the shop open in the evening in with the winds of change, altering work roles, procedures, policies and expectations; jobs have become more complex and highly demanding. in such a competitive work environment, one of the most common side effects of work life is stress. among the human services occupations, police work has been found to be the most stressful. in fact, psychologists agree that a police officer is under stress and pressure unequalled by any other profession. policemen can be viewed as social surgeons who make interventions in social situations to preserve law and order of the state. the job of a policeman has been said to be unique as one deliberately engages himself for which he is feared, hated and at times reviled or even assaulted in the ordinary performance of their duties .to carry out their job of maintaining law and order and engaging in investigations, they are required to be detached, unemotional, scientific and cautious .considering the nature and demands of their work, police personnel are certainly prone to experiencing stress. stress in such a profession can be debilitating not only to the individual but also to the police organisation and the community at large. at an individual level, studies have indicated that police officers experiencing stress reported poor physical and subjective health. stress was due to organisational factors rather than physical hazards involved in the police work. the factors included round clock duty, no time for family, inadequate salaries/facilities, negative interaction with other police staff, poor equipment, no recognition, too many cases, insufficient staff and harassment. lack of superiors interest in their subordinates, irregular office work, heavy workload and dealing with criminals were stressors faced by constables.
